_meta.framework: RIASEC Holland Codes (1973) + Big Five Personality Adaptation + Situational Judgment Tests

question_types.personality_trait: Big Five openness/conscientiousness markers adapted for career counselling

_meta.answer_key: A=0, B=1, C=2, D=3 (0-indexed for frontend convenience)

school_student.label: School Student (Class 9–12)
school_student.description: 15 RIASEC-grounded questions covering interest, aptitude, personality, cognition, and situational judgment.

questions.question: When you have completely free time on a weekend, which activity pulls you in most naturally and consistently?
questions.hint: Think about what you do without being told to.

options.text: Taking apart a gadget or building something mechanical / electronic / digital
Reading about discoveries, watching science documentaries, or running small experiments
Drawing, writing stories, composing music, designing something, or any creative act
Organising events, playing team sports, leading a group, or doing community work

questions.question: Which of these school situations makes you feel most alive, engaged, and genuinely confident?
questions.hint: Not just 'what you score highest in' — what you actually enjoy.

options.text: Cracking a complex Maths or Physics problem that took multiple steps and real effort
Running a Biology experiment or understanding how the human body or ecosystem works
Writing an essay, making a class presentation, or winning a debate with your arguments
Understanding how a business works, analysing markets, or solving an economics case

questions.question: When you face a brand-new challenging problem you have never seen before, what is your very first natural instinct?
questions.hint: Be honest — what do you actually do, not what you think you should do.

options.text: Break it into logical steps, work from first principles, solve it systematically alone
Hunt for patterns, analogies, or connections to something I have already solved before
Brainstorm unconventional or imaginative solutions — think outside the obvious approach
Immediately gather input from others, discuss it collaboratively, and build on their ideas

questions.question: In any group project or team activity, what role do you naturally and almost automatically fall into?
questions.hint: What role do others assign you — or you quietly take?

options.text: The researcher — gathering facts, verifying accuracy, making sure the content is deep and correct
The designer or presenter — making it visually compelling and persuasive for the audience
The coordinator — setting timelines, dividing tasks, keeping everyone on track and motivated
The technical builder — writing the code, building the prototype, or solving the hard technical piece

questions.question: If you could shadow any professional for one full week to experience their day-to-day life, who would you choose without hesitation?
questions.hint: Choose who genuinely excites you — ignore salary or status.

options.text: A scientist or engineer building AI systems, space technology, or cutting-edge robotics
A surgeon or doctor making life-or-death clinical decisions and healing patients
A startup founder or business leader building a company from zero to scale
An artist, filmmaker, game designer, or architect creating experiences for the world

questions.question: How do you genuinely feel when you encounter numbers, data tables, statistics, or mathematical proofs?
questions.hint: Be honest — not what sounds impressive.

options.text: Genuinely excited — I enjoy spotting patterns, calculating, and drawing logical conclusions from data
Comfortable — I can work with data fine, especially when it tells an interesting story or solves a real problem
Neutral — I prefer working with people, words, or ideas; numbers are a tool, not a passion
Challenged — I struggle with numbers and actively prefer subjects that rely on language or creativity

questions.question: What motivates you the most deeply when you think about your life's work — the career you want?
questions.hint: Choose the one that feels most genuine, not most admirable.

options.text: Solving problems that most people can't solve — being the technical or scientific expert people rely on
Making a real difference in people's lives — health, education, justice, or community impact
Building wealth and independence — growing a business or achieving financial success on my own terms
Creating something lasting — a work of art, a product, a scientific body of knowledge, or a company

questions.question: Your school receives an unexpected fund to build one major new facility. You are on the student decision committee. Which project do you champion — and why?
questions.hint: Pick what genuinely excites you, not what sounds most responsible.

options.text: A fully equipped tech lab with AI, robotics, 3D printers, and advanced computing for student projects
A community outreach centre connecting the school to underprivileged neighbourhoods and social causes
A student startup incubation lab where students can pitch and build real small businesses with mentors
A world-class creative arts centre with music studios, film editing suites, design labs, and a stage

questions.question: How do the people who know you best — friends, family, teachers — most consistently describe you?
questions.hint: What do they say about you, not what you aspire to be.

options.text: Intensely curious and analytical — always asking 'why' and diving deeper than anyone else
Warm and empathetic — people naturally come to me when they need support or honest advice
Ambitious and driven — I set big goals and relentlessly work toward them, inspiring others along the way
Creative and unconventional — I see things differently and consistently bring fresh, original ideas

questions.question: Which type of content do you voluntarily watch, read, or listen to during your own time — without anyone asking?
questions.hint: Think about your actual YouTube/podcast/book habits.

options.text: Science, tech, engineering, space, programming tutorials, or AI / maths content
Business stories, entrepreneur interviews, stock market / economics / startup content
Art, music, films, creative writing, design inspiration, cultural or philosophical content
Social issues, psychology, history, true crime, or content about people and society

questions.question: When you need to learn something completely new and complex, which approach feels most natural and effective for you?
questions.hint: Not what teachers tell you to do — what actually works for your brain.

options.text: Read systematically from first principles — fully understand the theory before touching practice
Jump in hands-on immediately — experiment, make mistakes, reverse-engineer how it works
Find a study group or mentor — I learn fastest by discussing, asking questions, and teaching others
Create visual summaries, mind maps, diagrams, or teach the concept to an imaginary student

questions.question: You discover that your city's public bus routing system is deeply inefficient — causing thousands of people to lose hours daily. How do you personally respond?
questions.hint: Be honest about your instinct, not what sounds most civic-minded.

options.text: I'd build a technical solution — write an algorithm or app that optimises the routes and submit it to authorities
I'd research the problem rigorously, document evidence with data, and publish a detailed analysis report
I'd mobilise the community — start a petition, organise affected citizens, and advocate loudly to decision-makers
I'd frame it as a business opportunity — research the market, pitch a mobility startup, and seek funding

questions.question: Which future academic path makes your heart race with genuine excitement and feels most authentically right for who you are?
questions.hint: Ignore parental pressure or peer comparison for a moment.

options.text: IIT / Engineering / Computer Science — becoming a world-class technical expert building tomorrow's systems
AIIMS / Medical / Biotech — becoming a healer, researcher, or innovator in life sciences
IIM / Business / Economics — understanding markets, building companies, creating financial independence
NID / Architecture / Fine Arts / Film — creating meaningful, beautiful, or culturally impactful work

questions.question: Ten years from now — at the peak of your career — what kind of life, work, and impact do you most deeply want?
questions.hint: This is not about what sounds impressive. What actually makes you come alive?

options.text: Recognised as a top domain expert — the person who knows more than anyone and breaks new ground
Running an organisation or leading a movement — visible, influential, making systemic impact
Famous for creating — a body of work in art, design, writing, or technology that people remember
Improving lives at scale — healthcare, education, policy, or social systems that genuinely help millions

questions.question: You have an entire summer with no obligations, no exams, and no family pressure. You can spend it any way you truly want. What do you actually do?
questions.hint: What would your ideal self do — given full freedom and no judgement?

options.text: Build a complete technical project from scratch — an app, website, robot, or experiment — that solves a real problem
Intern or volunteer in a hospital, NGO, school, or community organisation — work that directly helps people
Launch a small real business or side project — test a product, make sales, learn how the market actually works
Deep-dive into subjects I'm genuinely obsessed with — read voraciously, take online courses, expand my mind

parent.label: Parent of School Student
parent.description: 12 questions observing child behaviour to help parents guide rather than project career choices.

questions.question: When your child plays or spends free time at home, which activity absorbs them most deeply and consistently — without any prompting?

options.text: Building, fixing, coding, or engineering things — Lego, electronics, games, or computers
Drawing, writing stories, singing, dancing, or any form of creative / artistic expression
Reading books, watching educational content, asking deep questions about how the world works
Organising friends, leading games, planning activities, and naturally taking charge of situations

questions.question: In which school subjects does your child consistently score well AND show genuine enjoyment — not just compliance?

options.text: Mathematics and Science — gets high marks and seems energised, curious, and enthusiastic in these
Languages, History, and Social Studies — strong with words and fascinated by people and society
Commerce and Economics — interested in how businesses, money, and markets work
Art, Music, or Computer Science — drawn to creative expression and/or hands-on technical work

questions.question: When your child faces a difficult challenge or obstacle, what is their natural, characteristic response?

options.text: Persists quietly and independently — works methodically through it alone without giving up
Gets creative — tries unconventional approaches, improvises, and thinks outside standard methods
Mobilises others — gathers friends or family, discusses, and works collaboratively toward a solution
Strategises — makes a clear plan, identifies resources needed, and executes with focused determination

questions.question: If you had to honestly identify your child's single strongest natural talent — what you observe consistently — what would it be?

options.text: Exceptional logical and mathematical reasoning — they solve hard problems intuitively
Outstanding empathy and social intelligence — they understand people in ways that surprise adults
Natural leadership — they command respect, motivate others, and take charge without being asked
Vivid creativity and imagination — they produce original ideas and work that genuinely surprises everyone

questions.question: When your child talks about their future, which type of aspiration comes up most authentically and repeatedly?

options.text: Becoming a scientist, engineer, doctor, or expert who solves important technical or medical problems
Starting a business, becoming financially independent, or building something of their own
Working in a creative field — art, film, design, music, writing, or architecture
Helping people directly — becoming a doctor, teacher, counsellor, social worker, or government officer

questions.question: As a parent, which factor is most critical for you when considering your child's career path?

options.text: Job security and stable income — a clear, reliable professional path with predictable progression
High earning potential — careers with the best long-term income and financial independence prospects
Passion and fulfilment — my child doing work they genuinely love, even if it takes longer to pay off
Social prestige and recognition — a career that earns respect and status in our family and community

questions.question: How does your child typically approach homework, studying, or learning new things at home?

options.text: Self-directed and systematic — researches deeply, makes notes, and insists on fully understanding before moving on
Hands-on and experimental — prefers doing over reading, builds or tries things rather than studying theory
Social learner — studies best with friends, asks many questions, and explains things to family members
Creative processor — creates visual summaries, diagrams, or stories to internalise concepts

questions.question: How would you describe your child's relationship with risk-taking and ambitious goals?

options.text: Bold risk-taker — excited by challenges, not afraid of failure, loves trying big bold ideas
Calculated — thinks carefully before acting, analyses pros/cons, takes calibrated well-researched risks
Cautious — prefers stability and security, works best with clear structure and defined expectations
Team-driven risk-taker — confident when supported by the right people around them

questions.question: In social or extracurricular settings — school events, clubs, friends — what role does your child naturally take?

options.text: The quiet expert — respected for deep knowledge, sought out for advice, prefers quality interactions
The natural leader — initiates plans, organises people, energises the group, takes charge naturally
The creative spark — brings original ideas, entertains, designs, or creates things the group appreciates
The helper and mediator — supports others, resolves conflicts, ensures everyone feels included

questions.question: Which academic subject does your child go beyond the textbook in — reading extra books, watching videos, or practising voluntarily?

options.text: Mathematics, Physics, Computer Science, or Chemistry — the hard sciences and technical subjects
Biology, Psychology, Sociology, or Health — subjects about living systems, people, and well-being
History, Political Science, Economics, Law, or Current Affairs — society, power, and governance
Art, Literature, Music, Film, or Design — culture, expression, and aesthetic creation

questions.question: When your child disagrees with a teacher, friend, or authority figure, how do they typically handle it?

options.text: Researches and comes back with evidence — calmly argues with facts, data, or logical reasoning
Voices it confidently and directly — advocates for their view without being aggressive
Seeks compromise — tries to understand the other side and find a middle ground everyone accepts
Finds a creative workaround — doesn't confront directly but innovates around the constraint

questions.question: If your child could make any difference in the world — completely unbound by salary or practicality — what would they want their life's work to be about?

options.text: Advancing science and technology to solve humanity's biggest problems — disease, energy, climate, intelligence
Building businesses or systems that create jobs, prosperity, and economic opportunity for many people
Creating art, culture, or content that makes people feel understood, inspired, or less alone
Improving human welfare at scale — better healthcare, education, justice, or community resilience

college_student.label: College Student (Undergraduate)
college_student.description: 12 questions for undergraduates choosing specialisations, postgraduate paths, or career pivots.

questions.question: Looking honestly at your undergraduate studies so far, which aspect has created the deepest, most genuine engagement for you?

options.text: Technical coursework, labs, and building actual systems, products, or experiments
Research papers, case studies, theoretical models, and understanding mechanisms at depth
Group projects, presentations, networking, leadership roles, and the human dynamics of college
Electives in entrepreneurship, design, innovation, policy, or interdisciplinary cross-domain subjects

questions.question: What is your honest relationship with risk, uncertainty, and ambiguity in your professional choices?

options.text: I embrace risk energetically — uncertainty excites me and I am comfortable with ambiguity and changing plans
I take calculated risks — I move after thorough analysis and when the evidence strongly supports it
I prefer structured, predictable environments — I do my best work with clear roles and defined expectations
I manage risk through people — the right team gives me the confidence to make bold moves

questions.question: Which professional scenario, when you imagine it vividly, makes you most excited about your life 5 years from now?

options.text: A leading technical or research expert — published, respected in my field, solving problems nobody else can
Running my own venture — a startup, consultancy, or creative business I have built from the ground up
In a high-impact social role — policy, education, healthcare, diplomacy, or non-profit leadership
A senior role in a high-growth company — VP Product, VP Strategy, or partner at a top firm

questions.question: How do you work most effectively and produce your best output?

options.text: Solo, deep focus — give me a hard problem, full autonomy, and undisturbed time to solve it completely
Collaborative energy — I think and create best when bouncing ideas with smart, motivated people
Structured process — clear brief, defined milestones, regular check-ins, systematic execution
Creative exploration — open-ended briefs with freedom to experiment, iterate, and discover

questions.question: At the deepest level — what kind of impact do you want your professional life to have on the world?

options.text: Technological progress — advancing AI, engineering, medicine, or science that changes what is possible
Economic empowerment — creating jobs, building businesses, generating prosperity for many people
Human welfare — improving healthcare, education, mental health, or access to justice for underserved people
Cultural contribution — creating art, content, design, or stories that enrich how people experience life

questions.question: When you look at your peers honestly, in which area do you consistently outperform most of them without trying particularly hard?

options.text: Technical and quantitative skills — coding, problem-solving, maths, data, or engineering
Social and interpersonal skills — reading people, building relationships, persuading, and leading groups
Creative and design thinking — generating original ideas, visual sense, storytelling, or aesthetic judgment
Research and synthesis — finding information, connecting dots, writing deeply, building arguments

questions.question: You receive two job offers simultaneously. Offer A: lower salary but cutting-edge research role at a top lab. Offer B: higher salary but routine corporate job. What do you do?

options.text: Take Offer A without hesitation — the intellectual growth and long-term expertise matter far more than immediate money
Negotiate aggressively — try to get the best of both, counter both offers, and push hard on terms
Evaluate based on team and culture — which environment will help me grow, collaborate, and feel motivated
Consider a third path — use the time to launch something of my own rather than joining either

questions.question: Outside of your core academics, what activity or initiative defines you — the thing you would be known for on campus?

options.text: Technical side projects — hackathons, coding competitions, research papers, or open-source contributions
Student government, organising events, leading clubs, or driving campus initiatives
Creative expression — theatre, design, writing, music, film, or social media content
Social impact projects — NGO work, mental health advocacy, environmental activism, or teaching underprivileged students

questions.question: When you are genuinely passionate about a topic, how do you go about learning it?

options.text: I go extremely deep — read primary sources, research papers, original texts, and build mastery
I learn by building — start a project, make mistakes, and figure out everything I need as I go
I learn socially — find communities, attend events, take courses with others, and discuss constantly
I learn strategically — map what I need, find the best resource for each piece, optimise for outcomes

questions.question: Which one-line professional identity feels most authentically you — the person you are becoming?

options.text: 'The expert who solves the hardest technical and analytical problems in the room'
'The leader who builds and mobilises teams to create ambitious things together'
'The creator who makes things that move people, delight users, or change culture'
'The strategist who identifies opportunities, allocates resources, and drives profitable growth'

questions.question: How do you feel about the prospect of 2–5 more years of formal education (Master's, MBA, Ph.D) before entering or advancing in your career?

options.text: Eager — I want to go deeper intellectually and a research/academic path genuinely excites me
Strategic — I'll do an MBA or specialised master's only if it demonstrably accelerates my career trajectory
Undecided — I want to work first, gain real experience, and decide based on what gaps emerge
I'd rather skip formal education and dive straight into building something on my own

questions.question: Your college friend group is debating the single most important thing to optimise for in a first job. What position do you champion?

options.text: Learning velocity — find the place where you will grow the fastest, even if the pay is low
Compensation — maximise salary early because compounding is powerful and you can learn anywhere
Impact and mission — join a company whose work genuinely matters and aligns with your values
Network and optionality — go where you meet the best people, since relationships compound most

college_graduate.label: College Graduate (Career Transition)
college_graduate.description: 10 questions for recent graduates navigating job markets, postgraduate education, or career transitions.

questions.question: Looking back honestly at your academic and early professional experiences — what type of work created the deepest, most genuine engagement for you?

options.text: Technical and analytical problem-solving — the harder and more complex the problem, the more alive I felt
Collaborating with teams, influencing stakeholders, and driving collective outcomes through people
Creative work — designing, writing, building products, generating novel ideas, or visual storytelling
Research, deep analysis, synthesis, and producing insights that help people make better decisions

questions.question: At this stage of your life and career, what does personal and professional success genuinely mean to you?

options.text: Mastery — being the best at my craft, developing deep expertise that few others possess
Financial freedom — building the wealth and assets that give me complete autonomy and choice
Making a tangible positive impact — measurably improving lives, communities, or systems that affect people
Creating lasting work — a product, company, creative body of work, or idea that endures beyond me

questions.question: If you are considering a career transition, what is the primary driver of that change?

options.text: I want deeper technical or intellectual challenge — my current path feels too shallow or slow-moving
I want more autonomy and ownership — to build something I control rather than execute others' visions
I want more meaning — to work on something that genuinely helps people or contributes to society
I want better financial reward — my current trajectory doesn't match the compensation I know I can earn

questions.question: When you honestly audit your professional skills — what do you do noticeably better than most people you have worked with?

options.text: Technical execution — I build, code, model, or analyse faster and more accurately than my peers
Interpersonal influence — I read people, build relationships, and align groups behind a shared goal
Creative problem-solving — I generate original, unexpected ideas and solutions that surprise people
Research and strategic thinking — I find the best information, synthesise it, and present compelling arguments

questions.question: Which work environment would allow you to produce your absolute best work and feel most energised daily?

options.text: A research lab, engineering team, or technical organisation — deep work with brilliant domain experts
A fast-scaling startup or entrepreneurial venture — fast decisions, high ownership, diverse challenges
A social impact organisation, NGO, school, or healthcare system — meaningful mission, serving people
A creative studio, design firm, media company, or agency — where craft, aesthetics, and originality are valued

questions.question: How do you feel about pursuing postgraduate education — a Master's degree, MBA, or Ph.D — at this stage?

options.text: Strongly considering a Ph.D or research Master's — I want to pursue academic depth and original research
Considering an MBA or professional Master's — primarily to accelerate career trajectory and expand network
Not interested in more formal education — I learn best by doing, building, and gaining real experience
Open to a specialised technical Master's if it closes a specific skill gap critical to my target career

questions.question: How do you feel about management and people leadership as a significant part of your career trajectory?

options.text: Actively seeking it — I want to lead teams, scale organisations, and eventually run a function or company
Open but cautious — I want to lead if it serves the mission, but I equally value staying close to the actual work
Prefer the individual contributor track — I want to be the world's best at my craft, not manage others
Entrepreneurial leadership — I want to build my own team from scratch, not inherit an existing one

questions.question: Which industry or sector, when you think about working in it for the next 5–10 years, genuinely excites you most?

options.text: Technology, AI, deeptech, space, or advanced engineering — building the infrastructure of the future
Finance, consulting, VC, private equity, or professional services — high-leverage advisory and capital work
Healthcare, education, government, social enterprise, or international development — mission-driven work
Creative industries — media, entertainment, gaming, design, fashion, or brand — where culture is shaped

questions.question: If you had to commit to one concrete 90-day action right now to advance your career in the direction that excites you most, what would you actually do?

options.text: Build a portfolio project, get published, or achieve a technical certification to demonstrate hard skill depth
Start a side business, find co-founders, apply to accelerators, or test a business idea with real customers
Network aggressively in my target industry — 50 conversations, 5 informational interviews a week, build relationships
Research and apply to the best graduate programmes or fellowships that align with my intellectual interests

questions.question: At the end of a long career — looking back from age 60 — what would make you feel you lived and worked meaningfully?

options.text: I contributed to human knowledge or technology in a way that made what was impossible, possible
I built organisations that created prosperity, jobs, and economic opportunity for thousands of people
I touched and improved the lives of many individuals — through teaching, healing, counselling, or service
I created enduring work — art, stories, designs, or products that made life richer, more beautiful, or more human
